sentences.text: My name is Irene Schek, S-C-H-E-K.
I was born in Cracow, Poland, in 1921.
Now I live in Florida, Delray Beach.
Yes, I did.
I went to schools there, first the grammar school, and then junior high, and high school.
And the war started, of course, my education ended.
It was a suburb of Cracow where I lived.
So it wasn't as large and as populist as the Cracow itself.
But I would say that a few thousand must have lived there too, just the same.
[INAUDIBLE].
There were a lot of Jewish people, more religious and less religious, and reformed.
Plenty of synagogues.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On of the subjects in school was the Jewish religion, of course.
No, not until they formed a ghetto.
Up till that time, everybody was free to move wherever they wanted and [INAUDIBLE] in Cracow for that matter too.
Yes.
Most of them were educated people.
One of my uncles was a doctor.
Another one was a professor.
And their children were educated also.
So and some have stars.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They all earned a decent living.
Just two, my brother and myself.
George, [?
Yese ?] in Polish.
Schek.
My father's name was Leopold.
And my mother's name was [?
Sarah. ?]
Opperchingski, ?] you will never be able to spell.
This was her maiden name, [?
Opperchingski ?].
That's right.
Sheck, after her husband.
Sure.
My mother didn't work until my father passed away, of course.
Very few women, Jewish women, worked.
My father had a factory.
And they produced bathroom accessories, chrome-covered, very beautiful, hangers for towels, and for the tooth cups, and also for the foyer, hangers for coats.
They were also made out of the-- the same metal and finished with chrome, very shiny, beautiful.
So this was how he earned his living, really.
I finished high school, and the war started.
No.
What you would call, they were already reformed, because we didn't have two different sets of dishes.
And we observed the high holidays.
But my parents didn't go regularity to the temple, the synagogue, on every Friday.
They didn't, only on high holidays.
My mother never wore a wig.
So we were what you would call the reformed Jews already.
Did you-- did you observe Pesach?
Yes.
We had a different set of dishes.
And matzah was stored in a safe, dry place, and plenty of hard boiled eggs.
How-- how were your lives affected, you know, with-- with what was happening in Germany?
Did you hear anything?
Did you experience any acts of anti-semitism?
At this point, no.
But we heard on the radio what was happening in Germany, and what was happening in-- in part of Poland, Silesia, that was on the border of Germany already.
But, you know, you heard.
And you didn't want to believe that this can really happen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it did happen.
Before the--
Yes, of course.
There were only five Jewish girls in the classroom in the high school where I was going.
And some of them were nice, and others were anti-semites.
But--
They would make some comments, or you all stink, or you all-- I think it was purely jealousy because the Jewish girls were always smarter than the others.
They had better grades.
And they were dressed decently.
And the-- the Polish people were not so very, very-- how should I say-- aristocratic.
There was a part, of-- of course, that studied at the university.
And they were doctors, Polish doctors, and lawyers, and so on.
But the majority was not very well-educated-- very simple people.
Yes, but then we didn't have a way to go.
I just remembered that my brother got from a distant-- distant relative of our father an affidavit to come to United States that he never used anyway.
But this was the only meaning of getting out of Poland.
Some people did, the ones that had the good fortune of buying the papers of other countries, you know, and they escaped.
Yes.
There was a student organization when I was in high school, all through the high school.
Of course, it was not legal.
But everybody belonged anyway.
And for a very--
--short time, I belonged to a Zionist organization, very short time.
Akiba.
Oh, they prepared people to-- to go to Israel.
They had courses of-- in Hebrew, and had Hebrew songs, and on like Shabbat we had.
But I didn't belong for a very long time.
Not till the ghetto.
Yes.
When we were finally in the ghetto, they did.
Yes, but again, the same thing.
We didn't believe that this-- this can happen.
We thought it's a propaganda.
Somebody's just feeding information that are not true.
But it was true.
Can you remember the day?
Every Polish-- every Polish officer and-- and the top brass ran away.
This was the first.
And the poor guys, the poor peasant that had the horse and buggy, this was the one that-- that remained.
All went out of Poland to-- to Hungary, or whenever they could manage to smuggle themselves out.
It was in September.
But I don't remember exactly.
I was home.
I was home.
And I heard the first bomb flying.
And then I realized that the war started.
I was very unhappy, very upset.
First of all, the schools stop existing.
So this was the first immediate change.
And then the food was not the available.
Sometimes you would have to go early in the morning to get the bread because there was nothing left right from the beginning.
And then, of course, it got worse and worse.
For a little while, yes.
But my father was dead already then.
And my mother was running the-- the business with little help from my brother that really didn't live in-- in Cracow.
He lived and in Warsaw.
But when the war started, he came to Cracow to be with the family.
No, never as bad for non-Jews as it was for the Jews.
The Jews were right away, you know, singled out for punishment.
They used to grab Jews, Orthodox Jews that had big beards, and cut it with huge scissors, of course, not only the beard, but-- but the flesh with it too.
Yeah.
It stopped existing.
No.
There was no-- no material to-- to work with.
And the Polish people that work originally didn't want to stay anymore.
And they tried to become big entrepreneur that they were never measure up to be.
But they tried.
You know, they have no-- no--
Well, they started with certain rules.
You were not supposed to be on the street after dark.
And we had to wear an armband with the Magen David, star of-- of David on it.
And this was the beginning, really.
It's how they started, and a little by little.
Yes.
Yeah.
Oh, the people that--
There was still the Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get mind to how do you say it in English-- organization that was taking care of people that really had no means of supporting themselves.
So they were distributing food.
And then they-- I remember that I work in-- in-- one of the parts of the-- they call it the Jewish [?
Gamina. ?] They moved some people from other towns to Cracow.
And we were supposed to find apartments for them.

And so this is what we were doing also, looking, going half a day from one street to another and asking how many people in that apartment, whether we can move another couple or a few children with-- with family that was living there.
So this is what we were doing when the war started, really.
Not very much, to be honest.
No.

Sometimes they-- they would-- just pointed out if the Jewish woman was hiding someplace, or she might, the Polish woman, peasant, go out and scream that the Jewish girl was hiding in-- in-- in her building someplace.
They were not very helpful.
So they-- they told the Germans that the Jews were hiding.
Yeah, and the Polish police.
The Polish policeman was no different.
Yes, I did.
When I was in ghetto already, I heard the Polish woman screaming, a Jewish woman worked in here!
She just wanted to attract attention, somebody should follow her into the building.
But there were some that did hide the-- the Jewish, mostly the girls, even in the building next door to the one that-- that I lived.
She was a concierge, that woman.
And I knew that she was hiding Jewish young girls in her apartment.
There were very, very few.
They were very eager to grab what was left from Jewish homes and Jewish businesses.
And-- and they used to lie.
They used to say that the German took it away from them.
But they were the one that really disposed of many, many things.
Not right away.
It took a few months, really.
Before that, they just let people that they thought they would be using for certain reasons.
And the rest was supposed to go the suburbs.
So I went with my mother to suburbs, really.
It was a little suburb.
It was called [?
Borakfalinski ?], a little, little-- I couldn't even call it a town, because it was not a town.
And this is where we stayed.
My mother was still going here and there to the business to see whether she can sell anything.
And I learned to cook.
I became a cook in the family.
I used to make potato soup.
We rented a-- a room from a Polish peasant family.
And this is where we lived.
No.
My brother was already in ghetto.
When they make-- formed the ghetto, he right away went in.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't see him too often until we moved to ghetto also.
They told us.
They-- we were ordered to get out from those little towns around the Cracow and go to ghetto.
No.
No, because somebody else was already living in our apartment.
But we moved with my mother's sister.
My uncle was a doctor.
And they had a big apartment.
And I remember my mother and my sister were sleeping in his waiting room.
But it was good because we were not alone.
We had have our very close relatives with us.
I adore my mother's sister.
They-- they form what they called the [GERMAN], place where Jews used to report in the morning.
And we were being sent to unload coal from a huge truck or wash dishes someplace.
The German used to employ us at the beginning in that fashion.
No, of course not.
We had no other way to go.
There was no where to go.
I-- I had an armband on my arm.
And nobody would let me in.
There was no other way just go back to ghetto.
This was right away after maybe a year later than when the Germans came.
So it was like, 1940.
Mm-hm.
It was the fall, because I remember the Jewish holidays.
And they're usually fall.
Yes.
I was scared, absolutely scared.
I remember that huge gate to the ghetto, and the-- the German soldier that was standing at the door.
And that's it.
No.
No.
No.
Some people did go that wanted.
The German employed, they had special passes.
And they could go out.
And also, when somebody died, of course, they carried a body to the cemetery.
And I remember my mother passed away in ghetto.
And she was very well-liked.
And a lot of people followed her to the cemetery.
And the Germans decided that this was the last time they letting the Jews out for that purpose, because there are many soldiers that are being-- German soldiers, being killed.
And nobody follows them to the grave.
And here, one Jewish woman, and the whole ghetto practically walked after.
Yeah.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
It was a year later, maybe, a year and a half later.
No.
Well, always I remember there were people buying something and others selling.
And one day we didn't have enough money to buy the food.
So my brother said, you better sell something.
And a woman showed up.
[LAUGHS] And he bought a big, huge down comforter from-- from my apartment.
So I had money to buy food.
Yes, he was.
In the other room.
The apartment was big.
And-- but there were plenty of people in that big apartment.
Only through my aunt, my aunt that was my mother's sister.
And of course, her children were my cousins.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We tried to be together.
What did I do all day?
I was working.
I was working in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a factory where they were making uniforms, German uniforms.
And this was one of my jobs.
And another one was-- I remember where they were making cardboard boxes also for Germans.
And-- but nobody paid us for them.
Yes.
My experience is yes, because during one action like this, really a-- a moment will decide whether you will be alive or dead, I noticed a Jewish policeman that I knew from Cracow.
And I said to him, help me.
And when the German that was selecting people turned around, he pushed me to the good side.
So he saved my life, really, not ashamed that he did.
Walter Goldberg.
Berg.
Goldberg.
Yes.
[INAUDIBLE], was his name, like Mr.
Page.
[LAUGHS]
I don't know.
But I can tell you that he did save me.
That's for sure.
My mother was not alive anymore.
And my brother went out.
And he came back also after the selection.
So the family was shrinking.
But we still were together.
Yes, almost every week.
It was panic.
It was-- it was really-- because we knew that when I was working already in the factory making the German uniforms, we used to come home in the evening.
And we saw already German soldiers standing all around the ghetto.
We knew that the action was going to take place next day.
And it sure did.
So.
Yes.
They-- they were standing in rows.
And they were looking at your face.
If he likes you enough, so he push you on the good side.
If not, on the bad side.
And this is when the Walter Goldberg came in-- in and he helped me.
Unfortunately, there were some Jewish people among us that were very, very easy to be persuaded to be Jewish policeman, and not always behaved the right way.
So they are the ones directing Germans where to go and where to find Jews that were hiding, sorry to admit it.
There was at the beginning, yes.
Their role was very minimal.
They really had very little to do.
They maybe had a little bread to distribute to people.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, not very much.
No.
We did it, even among ourselves we [? would ?] sing songs.
But there was no entertainment.
No.
No.
Schools were closed right away.
The shuls were existing.
But it wasn't a very safe place to be in the shuls, because Germans know where to find Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They're very religious, were going, of course.
They were praying.
There was a small underground, mostly young people that belonged to a Zionist organization before the war.
The Akiba, I think I mentioned to you.
This was the only underground.
They smuggled some people, yes, through the sewers outside the ghetto.
I don't think so, no.
No.
No.
Well, they will shoot you right then and then on the spot.
They used to hit you.
Sure.
I think so.
Yeah, pretty soon after.
Yeah.
And the first day, of course, took the old people.
And I remember standing during one action.
And I said to myself, I have no more problems because there's nobody older than myself that's living anymore in-- in the building where I live.
My mother was dead.
And--
